coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a fluorescent nanoprobe for determination of sulfadiazine after its dispersive solid-phase extraction using magnetic nanocomposite sorbent
coredata.dc:creator: Dehghani Soltani, Maryam
Dadfarnia, Shayessteh
Haji Shabani, Ali Mohammad
Afsharipour, Roya
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive and non-toxic fluorescent nanoprobe has been designed for the determination of sulfadiazine. Sulfadiazine was separated and preconcentrated by dispersive solid-phase extraction based on the magnetic nanocomposite sorbent of oxidized nanocellulose/polyethyleneimine/nitrogen graphene quantum dots. The quantification of sulfadiazine was based on the decrease in the fluorescence of Ag2S@L-cysteine quantum dots. In optimal conditions, the fluorescence of Ag2S@L-cysteine quantum dots linearly decreases with the increase of sulfadiazine concentration within the range of 2.0–40.0 µg L−1. A limit of detection (LOD) of 0.41 µg L−1 and intra- and inter-day precision of 2.1%, and 4.3%, respectively, at the sulfadiazine concentration of 8.0 µg L−1 (RSD%, n = 7) were obtained. The maximum sorption capacity of the magnetic nanocomposite sorbent for sulfadiazine was found to be 39.0 mg g−1. The proposed procedure was successfully used to extract and quantify sulfadiazine from a variety of samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfadiazine
Fluorescent nanoprobe
Dispersive solid-phase extraction
Ag2S quantum dots
Nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nanocellulose and oxidized nanocellulose
Type of solvent on the fluorescence intensity of Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs
Effect of Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs volume and contact time with SDZ on the FL intensity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The quenching mechanism of fluorescence intensity of Ag2S@L-cysteine in the presence of SDZ.
General procedure for extraction and determination of SDZ.
(a) The FT-IR spectrum of Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs, (b) TEM image of Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs, and (c) The fluorescence emission spectrum of the nanoprobe.

The FT-IR spectra of oxidized nanocellulose, PEI, N-GQDs, and magnetic nanocomposite sorbent (a); FESEM image (b), elemental EDS analysis (c), XRD diffraction (d), and VSM analysis (e) of magnetic nanocomposite sorbent.

Response surface method and 3D graph: (a) pH and mass of the magnetic nanocomposite sorbent, (b) pH and desorption time, (c) eluent volume, and time sorption, (d) eluent volume and time desorption, (e) the magnetic nanocomposite dosage and time desorption.
Analytical features of the current method compared to other published SDZ determination procedures.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation; b Limit of detection; c Linear dynamic range; d Solid-phase extraction; e Dispersive micro-solid phase extraction; f Cloud point extraction; g Dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction;h Dispersive solid-phase extraction.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a fluorescent nanoprobe for determination of sulfadiazine after its dispersive solid-phase extraction using magnetic nanocomposite sorbent

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, 89195-741, Yazd, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, 89195-741, Yazd, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive and non-toxic fluorescent nanoprobe has been designed for the determination of sulfadiazine. Sulfadiazine was separated and preconcentrated by dispersive solid-phase extraction based on the magnetic nanocomposite sorbent of oxidized nanocellulose/polyethyleneimine/nitrogen graphene quantum dots. The quantification of sulfadiazine was based on the decrease in the fluorescence of Ag2S@L-cysteine quantum dots. In optimal conditions, the fluorescence of Ag2S@L-cysteine quantum dots linearly decreases with the increase of sulfadiazine concentration within the range of 2.0–40.0 µg L−1. A limit of detection (LOD) of 0.41 µg L−1 and intra- and inter-day precision of 2.1%, and 4.3%, respectively, at the sulfadiazine concentration of 8.0 µg L−1 (RSD%, n = 7) were obtained. The maximum sorption capacity of the magnetic nanocomposite sorbent for sulfadiazine was found to be 39.0 mg g−1. The proposed procedure was successfully used to extract and quantify sulfadiazine from a variety of samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfonamides (SNs), discovered in the 1940 s, were the first chemical compounds with actual antimicrobial properties employed in medicine and veterinary care. They are a major class of chemicals in the pharmaceutical companies that are frequently used in cancer therapy, anti-inflammatory, and antivirus medicines. Because of their widespread use and inexpensive cost, these drugs have found their way easily into the environment including underground water [1]. However, due to their physicochemical features, these compounds might accumulate in the ecosystem in a bioactive state causing a danger of antimicrobial resistance development. Sulfadiazine (SDZ) is a member of the sulfonamide family that has long-term negative consequences for global and public health, as well as a great number of resistant bacterial species [1,2]. This is a compelling cause to expand research analysis toward the development and implementation of selective and sensitive SDZ sensing technologies. For the determination of SDZ in biological matrices, different analytical strategies such as capillary electrophoresis (CE) [3,4], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [5–7], electrochemical techniques [8,9], chemiluminescence [10], and fluorescent sensors [11,12] have been used. Among these techniques, the fluorescent approach has piqued interest due to its ease of use, remarkable sensibility, inexpensiveness, and fast response. An important parameter in the design of fluorescence nanoprobes is the selection of highly sensitive and biocompatible material. Quantum dots are considered a substituent for conventional organic fluorophores because of their high luminescent and stable fluorescence. However, traditional QDs suffer from high toxicity and low sensitivity that limit their application in biological studies. Silver sulfide QDs is a member of the Ag-based QDs class that possess excellent photochemical resilience, non-toxicity, high quantum efficiency, and low background fluorescence [13,14]. Ag2S QDs have been used as an ideal fluorophore in the design of biosensors, bioimaging, and detection platform. For example, Nemati et al. synthesized silver sulfide quantum dots (Ag2S) using mercaptoacetic acid as the sulfur source and designed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor of Ag2S QDs and graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (g-C3N4) for the determination of cerium [13]. Yan and coworkers reported the synthesis of polyethyleneimine-capped Ag2S QDs using silver nitrate and sulfur dust in hydrazine for the detection of heparin and heparinase by fluorescence “turn on–off” method [15]. Shu and coworkers designed a near-infrared water-soluble emitter Ag2S QDs fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection and measurement of glutathione in human serum samples [16]. They synthesized the Ag2S QDs using mercaptoacetic acid as the sulfur source. Recently, Bao and coworkers prepared water-soluble silver sulfide quantum dots by microwave method using silver nitrate and D-penicillamine and designed a detection method for the quantification of dopamine [17]. Besides low toxicity, Ag2S QDs emit in NIR, reducing the autofluorescence and background scattering interference. Thus, based on the aforementioned discussion, Ag2S QDs can be introduced as a superb fluorescent sensor for the detection of trace amounts of some drugs, but its application as a fluorescent nanoprobe is still limited.

In this study, a new one-step, non-toxic, Ag2S@L-cysteine has been synthesized using the biocompatible amino acid of L-cysteine as the sulfur source. After characterization of the prepared QDs, a fast operation, and highly sensitive “turn-off” Ag2S@L-cysteine fluorescent nanoprobe has been designed for quantification of sulfadiazine.

Furthermore, as the concentration of SDZ in real samples is so low, and the matrices are complex, there is often a need for a separation and preconcentration step before its quantification by instrumental methods. Different methods of sample preparation like liquid–liquid extraction (LLE) [18], solid-phase extraction (SPE) [19], solid-phase microextraction [20] and dispersive solid-phase extraction [21,22] have been used for extraction of sulfonamides. Among these methods, dispersive solid-phase extraction has attracted much attention. One of the most significant tasks in the development of the dispersive solid-phase extraction technique is the choice of the proper and eco-friendly sorbent. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) [23], magnetic graphene oxide (Fe3O4@GO) nanocomposites [24], reduced graphene oxide [25], modified silica [25], magnetic molecularly imprinted polymer (MMIP) [21], and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [26] have all been utilized as sorbent materials in this regard. Among the sorbents used in dispersive solid-phase extractions, the biosorbent has gained specific interest owing to the application of low cost, stability, nontoxicity, and biodegradability [27]. In this regard, cellulose, a linear bio-polymer in living cells with the extraordinary properties of high surface area, mechanical strength, and high level of resilience is a promising sorbent [28,29]. Herein, through the combination of nanocellulose with polyethyleneimine, the amine groups of nanocellulose were increased, and then the oxidized nanocellulose/polyethyleneimine/nitrogen graphene quantum dots were prepared and utilized as the sorbent in a dispersive solid-phase extraction of sulfadiazine. The separated and preconcentrated sulfadiazine was then determined by the designed sensitive “turn-off” Ag2S@L-cysteine fluorescent nanoprobe as the result of the formation of hydrogen bonding between the functional groups of Ag2S@L-cysteine and sulfadiazine (Fig. 1 ).

Sulfadiazine antibiotic was purchased from the Alfa Aesar (Karlsruhe, Germany, https://www.alfa.com). Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA) provided the polyethyleneimine (PEI), ethylene glycol, silver nitrate (AgNO3, purity > 99.8%), L-cysteine, citric acid, iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate, urea, iron (III) chloride hexahydrate, ammonia solution (25%), hydrochloric acid, sodium chloride, and also acetonitrile (ACN). Other chemicals employed in this study were analytical grades provided by Merck Company (Darmstadt, Germany). Sulfadiazine standard solution (100 mg L−1) was made by mixing the necessary amount of sulfadiazine in a minimal amount of NaOH and diluting it to 100 mL with deionized water. Sulfadiazine working solution was made fresh every day by successive dilutions of the stock solution.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded by an Fl-Ar-2015 fluorescence array spectrometer (Teifsanje Tajhiz Pishraftheh Co. Ltd., Tehran, Iran). The pH was measured by Metrohm 827 digital (Herisau, Switzerland). The mixtures were stirred using a Direct Driven Stirrer (Finetech, Korea) with a glass rod. A magnetic field (1.2 T) was used to separate the magnetic particles. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR spectra) (4000–400 cm−1) were acquired using KBr pellets by Bruker Vector 22 FT-IR spectrophotometer to explore the right formation of magnetic nanocomposite sorbent (Bruker, Germany). Under a 15 kV acceleration voltage, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Sigma VP, ZEISS Sigma 300, Germany, https://www.zeiss.com) was used to examine the structural morphology of the produced nanoparticles. The STADI-MP (STOE Company, Germany) was used to perform X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) studies using monochromatized Cu Kα radiation. The elemental construction was examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Philips EM208S 100KV). The magnetism characteristics of the magnetic nanocomposite sorbent were confirmed using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) (LBKFB Kashan Desert Magnet Company, Iran).

The synthesis of Ag2S@L-cysteine quantum dots was performed according to the literature procedure with slight modifications [30]. Thus, first, 0.2 g AgNO3 was weighed and mixed with 4.0 mL of ethylene glycol as the solvent and 200 µL of 0.1 mol L−1 L-cysteine solution as the sulfur source at 110 °C under a nitrogen gas atmosphere. Then, to grow the core, the temperature was gradually increased to 150 °C. After a few minutes, the appearance of dark color indicates the synthesis of Ag2S@L-cysteine quantum dots is complete. The resulting mixture was cooled to ambient temperature and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 12 min. Finally, the resulting solution was collected and stored in a dark container at 4 °C for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nanocellulose and oxidized nanocellulose
ce:section.ce:para: The nanocellulose was prepared by acid hydrolysis of the cotton. For this purpose, first, 13.0 g of cotton was transformed into 400 mL sodium hydroxide solution (2 mol L−1) and stirred for 24 h with a mechanical stirrer at 70 °C to remove proteins, lignin, and hemicellulose from the cotton. The product was then washed with distilled water to neutral pH and dried in an oven at 50 °C. In the next step, 10 mL of 40% sulfuric acid solution was added to the prepared cotton and the mixture was stirred for 1 h at 50 °C. The surface of nanocellulose obtained at this stage is rich in hydroxyl groups. To oxidize nanocellulose and convert some OH groups to carboxylic acid, 10 mL of potassium persulfate (0.5 M) was added to 1.5 g nanocellulose and stirred for 16 h at 75 °C. The oxidized nanocellulose was then thoroughly washed with water and dried at room temperature [31].

The chemical coprecipitation method was used to prepare the magnetic oxidized nanocellulose. For this purpose, 1 g oxidized nanocellulose was added to 25 mL of deionized water and dispersed in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. Then 0.14 g FeCl3·6H2O and 0.08 g FeCl2·4H2O g were added and the mixture was kept under N2 atmosphere for 30 min. After that, the temperature of the mixture was raised to 80 °C and within 7 min, under vigorous shaking, 3 mL of NaOH aqueous solution (25% w/v) was added. The mixture was further heated at 80 °C for 90 min to complete the reaction. The black precipitates were then collected by a strong external magnet, washed properly with deionized water, and dried under a vacuum at 70 °C [32].

To prepare the magnetic nanocomposite, firstly nitrogen graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was synthesized via the “bottom-up” hydrothermal method. For this aim, 1.3 g citric acid along with 1.05 g of urea was added to 30.0 mL of deionized water and mixed for 5 min. The solution was autoclaved in a Teflon-lined stainless at 170 °C for 6 h [33]. After cooling, the resulting N-GQDs were centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 10 min to remove the large particles and then stored at 3 °C in a dark container. In the next step, 0.2 g of magnetic oxidized nanocellulose was transferred to a special synthesis container, 1 mL of polyethyleneimine was added to it, the lid was tightly closed, the temperature was raised to 60 °C, and was stirred for 30 min. Then, 10 mL of the prepared N-GQDs was added to it, the temperature was gradually raised to 80 °C and the mixture was stirred for 60 min under this condition. Finally, the precipitate was collected by a magnet, washed thoroughly with deionized water, and dried in an oven at 70 °C for 4 h.

The sorption experiment was performed in a glass beaker containing an aliquot of an aqueous sample or standard solution (with a maximum volume of 200 mL) containing up to 8.0 µg sulfadiazine (pH ∼ 7) and 30.0 mg of magnetic nanocomposite sorbent. After shaking the solution for 20 min, the sorbent was gathered at the bottom of the beaker by applying a magnet on the beaker's out wall and the supernatant was discarded. To desorb the analyte, 1.0 mL acetonitrile was added to the beaker and vortexed for 1.75 min, then, the magnetic nanocomposite sorbent was held in place by an external field (magnet 1.2 T), and the eluent containing SDZ was transferred to a proper vial for measurement. To determine the analyte, 500 µL ACN was added to 500 µL Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs in a quartz cell, followed by 500 µL separated SDZ, and the combination was rested for 15 min. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was then measured at the wavelength of 582 nm (F). In the same approach, the fluorescence (FL) intensity of a blank solution containing the same ingredient except SDZ was measured (F0). The ratio of ((F0-F)/F0) was used as the analytical signal for the determination of SDZ. The procedure is shown schematically in Fig. 2 .

Milk samples were prepared from a local supermarket. To 200.0 mL of the milk sample, 20 mL of an ethanol/acetic acid (97:3, v/v) solution was added and under sonication homogenized for 30 s. The resulting solution was centrifuged for 5 min at 5000 rpm. The supernatant was then treated according to the general procedure [34].
The well water sample (Yazd University, Yazd, Iran) was prepared by passing through a 0.45 µm diameter filter and treated according to the general procedure.

The synthesized Ag2S@L-cysteine as an optical sensor was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), fluorescence emission spectrum, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The FT-IR spectrum of Ag2S@L-cysteine (Fig. 3 a) contains O–H absorption bands at 3309 cm−1 and asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C–H in cysteine structure at 2940 cm−1 and 2877 cm−1, respectively. The C = O stretching of the carboxylic acid group might be assigned to the band at 1651 cm−1. Moreover, the band at 2050 cm−1 corresponds to the S–H in the L-cysteine structure. TEM image (Fig. 3b) showed that the prepared Ag2S@L-cysteine are monodisperse spherical nanoparticles. The fluorescence emission spectrum of Ag2S@L-cysteine was also considered. The fluorescence spectrum of Ag2S@L-cysteine (Fig. 3c) shows a strong emission peak in the range of 350–800 nm with a maximum of 582 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Type of solvent on the fluorescence intensity of Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Solvent has a crucial impact on fluorescence intensity. Solvent molecules can change the charge transfer process through rearrangement of their configuration or perturbation of the dielectric layer at the nanomaterial surface [35]. It is also observed that the n-level is stabilized and the FL intensity increases by increasing the solvent polarity. For this aim, the effect of four different types of solvents with different polarities including acetonitrile (3.92 D), acetone (2.88 D), ethanol (1.69 D), and methanol (1.7 D) on the intensity of QDs was investigated. Thus, 600.0 μL of the synthesized quantum dots were added to the 900.0 μL of each of the solvents and after 10 min, the corresponding fluorescence spectrum was plotted. The results of this investigation (Fig. S1) indicate that the FL intensity of Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs in acetonitrile with the highest polarity is higher. The greater effect of methanol (1.7 D) in comparison to acetone (2.88 D) can be related to its protic nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs volume and contact time with SDZ on the FL intensity
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs on fluorescence intensity was considered upon the addition of its various volume (100–600 µL) to 900.0 µL of SDZ (2 mg L−1) in ACN, and the total volume was adjusted to 1.5 mL with ACN. The FL intensity was measured after 8 min (F). The intensity of the same volume of Ag2S@L-cysteine in the absence of SDZ was also recorded (F0). The findings of the research (Fig. S2a) demonstrated that at a fixed amount of SDZ, the FL intensity was enhanced with an increase in QDs volume up to 500 µL, then reduces at higher volumes. The decrease in signal intensity at higher volumes can be related to the increase in the amount of Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs s relative to the SDZ concentration. For the determination of this antibiotic, 500.0 µL of water-soluble Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs were chosen.

The impact of contact time between SDZ and Ag2S@L-cysteine on the signal intensity was studied. For this aim, 500 µL of Ag2S@L-cysteine was added to 900.0 µL of SDZ solution with a concentration of 2.0 mg L−1, and the signal was measured in the contact time ranging from 0 to 20 min. It was found (Fig. S2b) that after 15.0 min, the signal intensity reaches its maximum. As a result, 15.0 min was chosen as the best contact time between the SDZ and the fluorescence nanoprobe.

The FT-IR spectra of the resulting magnetic nanocomposite sorbent along with polyethyleneimine, oxidized nanocellulose, and NGQDs are shown in Fig. 4 a. In the nanocellulose and magnetic nanocomposite sorbent spectra the absorption bands at 1334 cm−1 and 1163 cm−1 are related to stretching vibrations of C–O–C and C–OH, respectively. The wide absorption band in the region of 3413 cm−1 in the graphene quantum dots and magnetic nanocomposite sorbent is related to the O–H vibration, indicating the presence of graphene quantum dots in the sorbent. The absorption band at 3341 cm−1 of PEI related to N–H stretching also appears in the magnetic nanocomposite sorbent. Moreover, the absorption bands at 2856 cm−1 and 2900 cm−1 related to symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of C–H groups, respectively, appear in spectra of the nanocellulose and magnetic nanocomposite sorbent. The absorption bands at 1600 cm−1 and 1718 cm−1 correspond to the starching vibrations of C = C and C = O groups of the aromatic ring, also, are present in both spectra of the graphene quantum dots and magnetic nanocomposite sorbent. The FT-IR spectrum of magnetic nanocomposite sorbent also shows an absorption band at 556 cm−1 which corresponds to the Fe/O vibration. All of these bands collectively confirm the correct synthesis of the magnetic nanocomposite.

The morphology of the produced magnetic nanocomposite was determined by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). The synthetic nanocomposite (Fig. 4b) has a spherical shape. Furthermore, elemental EDS analysis (Fig. 4c) and the mapping of magnetic nanocomposite sorbent (Fig. S7) showed the presence of the elements Fe, O, C, and N in the matrix, and according to EDS analysis, they have a distribution of 19.3, 15.7, 61.9, and 3.2%, respectively.

The X-ray powder diffraction measurements were used to determine the crystalline structure of the magnetic nanocomposite, as shown in Fig. 4d. The XRD patterns of the prepared sorbent show diffraction peaks at 2θ of 30.82, 35.42, 38.87, 44.07, 54.47, 57.87° related to the Fe3O4 pure cubic spinel crystal in it. The diffraction peak at 21.17° confirmed the presence of PEI and N-GQDs in the sorbent structure. The peaks at 15.53, 17.36, and 23.53° are due to the presence of oxidized nanocellulose in the synthesized ONCs-Fe3O4@PEI/N-GQDs structure. The average grain size of magnetic nanocomposite sorbent determined by the Scherer equation was found to be 14.5 nm. The VSM analysis was used to consider the magnetic property of the synthesized sorbent. The S-like magnetic hysteresis loop of the magnetization curve with the magnetization value of 41 emu g−1 (Fig. 4e) proves its superparamagnetic property, making it adequate for rapid isolation from the solutions by a strong magnet.

The experiment design is a statistical and mathematical strategy that involves modeling and optimizing reaction variables to increase production efficiency, minimize time, account for variable interactions, and reduce overall error with the fewest number of runs. The response surface method (RSM) is a set of numerical methods for matching experimental results with polynomial methods in different ways. One of these methods is central composite design (CCD) consisting of full,
1 2
fraction, and small experimental designs that provide a reliable methodology.

In this work,
1 2
fraction mode of CCD with 5 factors including (pH (X1), the mass of magnetic bio-nano sorbent (mg) (X2), time of sorption (min) (X3), time of desorption (min) (X4), and volume of eluent (µL) (X5) at 5 levels was selected to obtain reliable results with a low number of runs. The overall number of runs (N) was calculated to be 32 using the formula N =
1 2
2
K
+ 2 K +
N 0
, where K refers to the number of parameters and
N 0
is the central point’s number (Table S1). After that, the central points were used to determine the data repeatability and experimental error. The following second-order polynomial formula was used to fit the CCD analysis data [36]:
(1)
Y =
b 0
+
∑
i = 1
n
b i
X i
+
∑
i = 1
n
b
ii
X
i
2
+
∑
1 = 1
n - 1
∑
j = i + 1
n
b
ij
X i
X j
+
e i
where, Y is the obtained response, bi , bii , and bij , symbolize the linear, quadratic, and interaction coefficients, respectively, b0 denotes the intercept, ei is the residual error, and Xi and Xj are coded variables. The significance of individual components and their interaction was statistically tested using analysis of variance (ANOVA). The terms in the model were also statistically tested to discover the best model by obtaining the Fisher test value (F-value) at a P-value of less than 0.05. Based on the values of the determination coefficient (R2), predicted determination coefficient (Pred-R2), adjusted determination coefficient (Adj-R2), and appropriate precision (Table S2, S3), the following quadratic polynomial was chosen as the best model.

The critical function of the response surface (RS) is to illustrate the link between independent factors and their responses. According to Fig. 5 a and b, by increasing the pH of the solution to 7 extraction efficiency is enhanced, and the maximum efficiency is achieved at pH ∼ 7. This observation can be related to the pKa values of SDZ (pKa1 = 1.57, pKa2 = 6.5). Thus, in acidic conditions, the amino groups of SZD are protonated, resulting in a decrease in its affinity for interaction with the carboxyl functional group of the sorbent. But with the increase of pH to 7, the concentration of H+ is reduced, and thereby the interaction between SDZ and ONCs-Fe3O4@PEI/N-GQDs is increased. However, in alkali conditions, the functional groups on the surface of the nanocomposite sorbent are deprotonated resulting in a decrease in its ability to hydrogen bonding with the SDZ antibiotic. Thus, according to the experiments, a pH ∼ 7 was selected for the next experiments. The sorption time is a crucial component that influences extraction recovery. After 20 min, the analyte sorption to the magnetic nanocomposite was completed and reached equilibrium (Fig. 5c). Fig. 5c and d indicate that 500 µL of eluent is sufficient for complete SDZ recovery from the magnetic nanocomposite sorbent. Desorption time has also a significant impact on the recovery of the analyte from the sorbent. Fig. 5b and d demonstrate that at a desorption period of 1.75 min, the recovery approaches its maximum level and becomes constant at a higher desorption time. The sorbent amount utilized is also a factor that can impact analyte recovery. Fig. 5e shows that for quantitative extraction of the SDZ, 30.0 mg of the prepared magnetic nanocomposite is sufficient. Thus, the optimum parameters for the extraction of SDZ with the prepared sorbent were: pH of the solution, ∼ 7; the amount of magnetic nanocomposite, 30.0 mg; eluent volume, 500 µL, sorption and desorption time, 20, and 1.75 min, respectively.

The ionic strength influence on SDZ extraction was investigated through the extraction of the sample solutions with a constant amount of SDZ and varying concentrations of sodium chloride (0.01–2.0 mol L−1). The results (Fig. S3a) showed that the existence of the NaCl salt does not affect SDZ extraction up to a concentration of 0.9 mol L−1 but at higher salt concentrations the extraction of SDZ decreases, which could be due to the limitation of mass transfer of SDZ into the magnetic nanocomposite sorbent in a high salt matrix.

The volume of the sample is another significant parameter that influences the preconcentration factor (PF) and demonstrates the extraction potential for the enrichment of low amounts of analyte from a large sample volume. PF is increased by increasing the volume ratio of the aqueous phase to the eluent, although the extraction efficiency may be reduced. The extraction method was performed on sample volumes ranging from 50.0 to 300.0 mL containing 1.0 µg of SDZ. The results (Fig. S3b) reveal that extraction efficiency was constant up to 200.0 mL, and decreased as sample volumes were further increased. As a result, a volume of 200.0 mL was chosen as the maximum volume for further studies.

The sorbent capacity was determined by adding 30.0 mg of magnetic nanocomposite sorbent under optimized conditions to 200 mL of SDZ solution and stirring the mixture with a mechanical stirrer for twenty minutes to guarantee the achievement of equilibrium. The sorbent was then held by a magnet and the remaining SDZ in the supernatant was measured. The magnetic nanocomposite sorbent capacity determined by the difference between the amounts of SDZ in the initial and final solution was found to be 39.0 mg g−1. Furthermore, the capability of the ONCs-Fe3O4@PEI/N-GQDs for the extraction of sulfadiazine was compared with ONCs-Fe3O4 and ONCs-Fe3O4@PEI and the results show that the extraction efficiency of ONCs-Fe3O4@PEI/N-GQDs for of sulfadiazine is higher than ONCs-Fe3O4 and ONCs-Fe3O4@PEI/N-GQDs (Fig. S4).

The method's selectivity was tested by extracting and measuring SDZ as well as other drugs including carbamazepine, lamotrigine, metformin, and phenobarbital. For this aim, 200.0 mL of 15.0 µg L−1 SDZ solution and any of the other drugs with a concentration of 150.0 µg L−1 were individually extracted and determined. The results (Fig. S5) indicate that in comparison to SDZ, the extraction efficiency for other drugs with a concentration ten times SDZ is insignificant. Thus, the devised SDZ extraction and measurement method offers a high selectivity.

After optimizing various beneficial factors, the suggested method's figures of merit were assessed, including precisions, linear range (LR), the limit of detection (LOD), the limit of quantification (LOQ), and the enhancement factor. The linear calibration graph with the sample volume of 200.0 mL for extracting SDZ at five concentration levels ranging from 2.0 to 40.0 µg L−1 has the equation of Y = 0.0026X + 0.005 (where Y is the analytical signal defined as ((F0-F)/F0) and X is the drug concentration in the aqueous phase (µg L−1)) and coefficient of determination (r2) of 0.9996 (Fig. S6). The preconcentration factor (the ratio of initial sample volume to the final extract) and enhancement factors (EF) (the ratio of the slopes of calibration curves with and without preconcentration) were 200.0 and 197.5, respectively, indicating the extraction of SDZ is quantitative (>98%). The relative standard deviation was used to assess the strategy's precision for standard solutions containing 3.0, 8.0, and 30 µg L−1 of SDZ (N = 7) (RSD%). Inter-day relative standard deviations were 4.8, 4.3, and 3.5%, while intra-day RSD were 4.2, 2.1, and 2.0%, respectively. The limits of detection and quantification were determined to be 0.41 and 1.38 µg L−1 using the equations of LOD = 3sb/m, and LOQ = 10 sb/m, respectively, where sb is the standard deviation of seven replicate blank signals and m is the slope of the calibration curve. Table 1 compares the validation parameters acquired using this method to those published by other researchers. The LOD of the designed method is lower than other compared methods and consequently, the linear range started at a lower concentration. The RSDs of this study are also better or comparable to other methods.

The described method was used to extract and determine SDZ in well water and cow milk samples. The reliability of the procedure was tested through recovery experiments by spiking the samples with three different concentrations of the analyte. The results (Table 2 ) revealed that the recoveries of the spiked samples are within the range of 96.3–104.0% indicating that the matrices of the considered samples have no effects on the determination of SDZ and the accuracy of the method is acceptable.

Silver quantum dots were synthesized and used as a nanoprobe for the measurement of sulfadiazine based on its quenching effect on Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs fluorescence. The Ag2S@L-cysteine QDs unlike the traditional QDs exhibited effective photophysical properties without releasing harmful metal ions into the environment. Trace amounts of sulfadiazine, an environmental pollutant, were isolated from cow milk and well water samples by dispersive solid-phase extraction using a synthesized magnetic nanocomposite sorbent (ONCs-Fe3O4@PEI/N-GQDs) before its measurement with the designed fluorescence nanoprobe. The structural properties of the quantum dots and the synthesized magnetic nanocomposite sorbent were investigated by various methods (FT-IR, FE-SEM, VSM, and XRD). The proposed method has the advantages of low detection limit, wide applicability, good selectivity, cost-effectiveness, non-toxicity, and ease of operation. Furthermore, the sorbent and the measurement method are biocompatible.
Author statement.

All authors contributed to the study's conception and design. Material preparation, data collection, and analysis were performed by Maryam Dehghani Soltani, Shayessteh Dadfarnia, and Ali Mohammad Haji Shabani, and Roya Afsharipour. The first draft of the manuscript was written by Maryam Dehghani Soltani and all authors commented on previous versions of the manuscript. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108642.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
